front: Play: Alec Baldwin's character yells 'Coffee's for closers!' and shows off brass balls in the film adaptation
Play: the first act is set in a Chinese restaurant where Aaronow and Moss mock selling to someone named Patel
Play: detective Baylen takes a salesman nicknamed 'The Machine' in for questioning after he admits to stealing leads
Play: the top salesman Ricky Roma first appears talking about the smell of train compartments; loses a sale to James Lingk
Play: Don and Teach plan to rob a customer who got a good deal on a rare coin from a junk shop
Play: Teach harbors a grudge about Ruthie saying 'Help yourself' after he stole a piece of toast from Grace
Play: titled from a Norwegian folk song; inspired by the Clarence Thomas hearings; Carol accuses professor John of sexual harassment
Play: Doug Brown's movie is green-lit over a radiation apocalypse film despite activist Karen's efforts
Play: Teach hits Bobby over the head with a 'dead pig sticker' after Fletcher fails to arrive; characters argue whether the mugging victim was taken to Columbus or Masonic Hospital
Play: Carol accuses professor John of sexual harassment and attempted rape after he offers to raise her grade to an 'A'; the language of professor and student switch in complexity over three scenes
Play: title from a Middle English poem asking God to 'speed' a farm implement; features movie producer Bobby Gould
Playwright: developed the 'practical aesthetics' approach to acting with William H. Macy at Goddard College
Playwright: adapted Chekhov's Uncle Vanya; Andre Gregory rehearsed this version in abandoned spaces, shown in Louis Malle's film Vanya on 42nd Street
Playwright: wrote the screenplays for Wag the Dog (Barry Levinson) and The Untouchables (Brian de Palma)
